game_name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: The Arcade Game

danger_signals.visual_form: Color-coded Foot Soldiers in distinct outfits (purple, etc.)
danger_signals.meaning: Different enemy types telegraphed by uniform color; threat type varies by appearance

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger sprite scale, unique character designs (Rocksteady, Bebop, Shredder, Krang)
danger_signals.meaning: Significant threat; major combat challenge ahead

danger_signals.visual_form: Exploding oil drums, traffic cones, parking meters rendered as interactive objects
danger_signals.meaning: Destructible environment signals danger zones; objects can be weaponized

danger_signals.visual_form: Specific Foot Soldier variants and Roadkill Rodney robots with distinct appearance
danger_signals.meaning: These enemies restrain player movement and drain health; heightened danger state

danger_signals.visual_form: Player health meter (implied NES HUD standard)
danger_signals.meaning: Direct feedback of survival threat level

safety_signals.visual_form: Scene transitions and stage boundaries (8 scenes/levels total)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone marker; brief respite between major threats

safety_signals.visual_form: Food/power-up objects dropped from defeated enemies
safety_signals.meaning: Health restoration and temporary invulnerability available
Player agency in selecting defensive/offensive playstyle; choice signal safety

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Turtle shell sprites, individual weapon silhouettes (katanas, staff, nunchaku, sai)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Urban New York setting; sewer/city level design consistent with hero territory

faction_indicators.color_identity: Purple primary color-coding for standard soldiers; variants in other colors
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Foot Soldier uniform design; standardized hostile NPC appearance
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Color-coded by attack pattern and weapon (standard purple, other variants with different threat profiles)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appears throughout all levels; indicates Shredder/Krang domain of control

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Shredder, Krang, Rocksteady, Bebop as visual authority figures; Baxter Stockman, Granitor, General Traag
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each boss has unique sprite/design; visual hierarchy above standard Foot Soldiers
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Boss encounters at level endpoints; Technodrome cutscene (Scene 5/7) signals faction stronghold

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Kidnapping narrative event (April O'Neil, Splinter captured)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Story cutscenes and attract mode showing opening cartoon sequence with theme song
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turtles chase through escalating levels; enemy density and boss difficulty increases as narrative progresses

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Foot Clan occupation of urban territory
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: City streets, dojo, warehouse environments saturated with purple-clad Foot Soldiers
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies respawn in waves; hostility constant; no friendly NPCs visible in occupied zones

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Technodrome approach (Scene 5 in arcade; Scene 7 in NES)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Planned cliffside visual in cutscene; dramatic environmental transition (though cliffside was cut from NES)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters intensify; enemy variety increases; narrative escalation signal

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shredder as final boss sprite; distinct from all prior encounter designs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ultimate threat behavior; game completion condition tied to Shredder defeat

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Regional gameplay differences (Japan vs Western versions)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Identical sprite rendering but different UI (high score display in Japanese version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Japanese version: fewer enemies, more sophisticated scoring (type-dependent points), 3 HP Foot Soldiers; Western: higher enemy count, flat 1-point scoring, 4 HP Foot Soldiers; Japanese: jump kick timing and special attacks mechanically enhanced

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Cheat options enable God Mode, Starting Lives adjustment, invincibility overlay (if activated)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy behavior unchanged visually but damage output and player vulnerability state shifts with cheat/difficulty settings

environmental_storytelling.technique: Interactive destructible objects as environmental narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: Traffic cones, parking meters, fire hydrants, exploding oil drums scattered throughout urban levels; players can weaponize environment to defeat enemies faster, signaling civilian infrastructure under siege

environmental_storytelling.technique: Scene progression as linear narrative visualization
environmental_storytelling.example: Eight scenes (arcade) / multiple scenes (NES) tracking geographic and narrative progression from street-level Foot Clan encounters toward Technodrome; visual journey echoes 'chase' narrative

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss character design as faction authority visualization
environmental_storytelling.example: Rocksteady and Bebop appear individually then together; visual progression of escalating threat; each boss has unique sprite identity reinforcing individual threat profile
Purple Foot Soldiers vs. other color variants; each color telegraphs attack pattern and weapon type without UI explanation; visual language teaches player enemy taxonomy
Game shows cartoon opening sequence + theme song during idle attract mode; embeds TMNT animated series lore and tone into arcade experience; franchise continuity maintained visually

environmental_storytelling.technique: Turtle weapon and stat silhouettes as class identity
environmental_storytelling.example: Leonardo balanced, Donatello long-range/slow, Michelangelo/Raphael fast/short-range; weapon sprite differences visible in selection screen; visual design communicates playstyle before character selected

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engage players on sight; respond to proximity triggers; typical melee combat
Execute distinct attack patterns based on color; telegraph different danger profiles to experienced players
Restrain player movement and drain health simultaneously; leave player vulnerable to additional enemy attacks; higher tactical danger
Appear individually first (encounter order), then together in later encounter; escalating threat signature
Unique sprite and behavior; game completion condition; ultimate threat manifestation
Boss encounter with distinct visual design; signals narrative escalation toward final confrontation
Jump mechanic, attack button responses, wall-spring mechanics; vary by character class (range, speed, attack speed differences across Leonardo/Donatello/Michelangelo/Raphael)

Jump kick can be performed anywhere during jump arc (not just descent); special attack by holding attack + jump; post-attack directional switching for aim adjustment; mechanically richer behavioral language

notes: TMNT II operates on a color-coded threat taxonomy and environmental interactivity model. Danger is signaled primarily through enemy appearance variants (Foot Soldier colors), boss sprite uniqueness, and environmental hazards. Safety is communicated through level transitions, item drops, and character selection agency. The Foot Clan faction occupies all visible territory, creating a unified antagonist visual language. Regional variants (Japan vs. Western) preserve visual identity but modulate mechanical difficulty and feedback richness. The attract mode embeds series lore. Enemy color-coding is a primary manifestation layer: purple = standard threat, other colors = tactical variants. Boss progression (individual → paired → final) creates narrative escalation through visual repetition.
